# Questions in Livestream 264

Live Stream #264: The Shasu of Vancouver

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=1476" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:36</a> | Is it better to wait for all the books in a series or read them as they come out? | He advised reading them as they release, especially while the series is still actively being written. | He answered from the perspective of an author still producing the series: readers should read the books as they come out. He also noted that the next book was still changing, even after beta readers, so waiting for everything would mean waiting on an active writing process rather than a finished set. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=1714" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:34</a> | If one could choose only one vocation, should it be artist, writer, painter, musician, or scholarship? | He said the practical question is what kind of poverty one is willing to accept, then cautioned that scholarship is not usually a good way to earn a living unless chosen carefully. | He framed the choice practically rather than romantically: both art and scholarship are financially hard, with artists likely to be "barista poor" and scholars even worse off. He said he does not recommend scholarship as an income strategy and added that the broader moment is also difficult for musicians and artists. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=1913" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:53</a> | What are his thoughts on grilled cheese sandwiches? | He likes them and suggested combinations such as brie with fruit and balsamic or honey, while emphasizing good melting cheese. | He called grilled cheese delicious when made with the right bread and cheese, then suggested more gourmet versions such as brie with strawberries and balsamic or brie with blueberries and honey. His main criterion was not a single recipe but using ingredients that melt and pair well. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=1973" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:53</a> | Butter or mayo for grilled cheese? | He said both can work depending on what one is doing. | He said butter and mayo can both be good depending on the sandwich. He then broadened the answer by emphasizing that grilled cheese depends more on melting properties, naming provolone, Swiss, mozzarella, cheddar, and smoked gouda favorably while warning against blue cheeses because they do not melt well for this use. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=2136" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:36</a> | What book would he recommend on Gnosticism? | He recommended starting with his own book on ancient Egypt and the origins of Gnosticism, with the caveat that the best entry point depends on prior background. | He recommended his own book, describing it as a primer that surveys theories of Gnosticism, their strengths and weaknesses, and his conclusions. He added that choosing a general primer is difficult because Gnostic studies has deep disagreement between camps, so prior background matters. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=2268" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:48</a> | Is Ezekiel 38-39 about modern Israel? | He said it concerns a future Israel, but because apocalyptic literature is often reinterpreted for later contexts, the text does not let him identify it confidently with present-day Israel. | He treated Ezekiel 38-39 as apocalyptic literature and said that makes the question difficult. It may relate to modern Israel, but it may instead refer to a later future Israel, since apocalyptic texts are repeatedly reinterpreted for present contexts and the passage itself does not settle which future setting is meant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=2383" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:43</a> | Are there different levels of hell? | He said the Bible is silent on that; later literature such as Dante influenced the idea, but there is no clear biblical warrant. | He said Scripture does not give enough information to affirm levels of hell. The popular idea comes especially from Dante's Inferno and later theological imagination, so he warned against confusing powerful literature with biblical fact. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=2607" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:27</a> | Is physical media for video games and media in general dead or dying? | No. He said physical media is seeing a revival as buyers react to digital purchases being removed or controlled by platforms. | He argued physical media is not dead because customers have seen digital books, music, and other purchases disappear when platforms or publishers pull access. That loss of control has driven renewed interest in CDs, records, cassettes, and physical books, which cannot be remotely removed or bricked in the same way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=2766" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">46:06</a> | Will Archive.org remain available in light of recent lawsuits? | He thought it likely survives, though he noted legal pressure on multiple archival platforms and some uncertainty about outcomes. | He distinguished Internet Archive from Anna's Archive and said the answer depends on which one is meant. In his view, Anna's Archive is harder to stop because it lacks a fixed physical presence, while Internet Archive has offices and servers that make legal judgments easier to enforce, so it may survive but not unchanged. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=2890" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:10</a> | What caused the programming language assembly to become extinct? | He said assembly is difficult, verbose, poor for large modular projects, and now mostly limited to boot code. | He said assembly faded because even simple work takes a great deal of code and modular programming is almost impossible at scale. In his view it is now mostly confined to boot code, and even that area has become standardized enough that little new assembly is written. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=2950" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:10</a> | What caused the programming language Perl to become extinct? | He said Perl is difficult to debug, and Python displaced it because programmers could spend more effort coding and less effort debugging. | He blamed Perl's decline on how easy it is to write obfuscated code and how hard even simple Perl can be to debug. He contrasted that with Python, which he found much easier to debug, and said he moved away from Perl after writing a large amount of it professionally. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=3065" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:05</a> | What are his thoughts on point-and-click adventure games? | He said they have their place and there is nothing wrong with them, though they are not generally his preference. | He said point-and-click adventure games are not generally his thing, but he did not dismiss them. He noted that many game interfaces use point-and-click interaction somewhere, such as maps, so the mechanic itself is ordinary and useful even if the genre is not his preference. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=3115" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:55</a> | What video-editing program does he use, and why? | He uses Adobe Premiere Pro largely because it is the tool he has used since college and already knows well. | He said he uses Adobe Premiere Pro because it is the tool he has long experience with, going back to college, and it works for him. He has heard DaVinci works well, but he has not tried it himself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=3148" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">52:28</a> | What storage platform does he use, and why? | He avoids cloud storage, disables it when possible, and prefers local/NAS storage because of privacy, control, and terms-of-service concerns. | He said he does not use cloud storage because providers can remove access, view data, suffer breaches, or change terms. He disables cloud integration on his PCs and in Word where possible because his work can be sensitive and he wants control over where files live. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=3267" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:27</a> | What does he do when he has to change computers? | He uses a NAS and copies data to or from it when moving between machines. | He said his NAS holds most of his data, while the most sensitive material may also live on a mirrored desktop drive. When changing computers, he copies segregated PC data to the NAS, sets up the new machine, and copies the data back. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=3330" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:30</a> | Is a NAS like a flash drive? | No. He described it as network-attached storage, essentially a small mirrored storage array accessible over the network. | He explained that a NAS is network-attached storage, not a flash drive: it behaves like a network share but is a device containing several mirrored hard drives. He uses a Synology unit because it is easy to set up and avoids the extra work of building a custom NAS. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=3538" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:58</a> | Does the destruction date of Hazor seem suspicious because of two Philistine sherds associated with Moshe Dothan? | He said not necessarily; archaeology is corroborative, and later work at Hazor matters more than attacking a result because of the excavator. | He said the issue should not be judged only by Dothan or the two sherds because archaeology is corroborative. What matters is whether later excavators and the broader body of work at Hazor confirmed the dating, and he indicated that subsequent work had done so. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=3716" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:56</a> | Could Joseph have risen to power in Egypt even without Hyksos rule? | Yes. He said Egypt sometimes elevated Asiatic Semites to very high office even outside the Hyksos period. | He said Joseph could plausibly have risen to power outside a Hyksos context because Egyptians sometimes allowed Asiatic Semites to become high officials, including vizier-level figures. Hyksos rule would make the situation easier because they were Semitic, but he did not think it was required. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=4140" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:09:00</a> | Are there any updates on the so-called curse amulet? | He said the topic is effectively dead and no longer looks viable as a serious line of evidence. | He said the last major defense he had heard was years earlier and that a proposed revised publication effort had stalled. Looking at the presented scans, he said the reading was not there, and he connected the stalled project to personnel and publication problems rather than fresh evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=4802" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:20:02</a> | Has he known PhDs who were incompetent in their own field or outside their expertise? | Yes. He said he has met scholars who were badly out of their depth despite holding the credential. | He answered yes and gave a sharply negative example of a scholar whose work he regarded as deeply incompetent. His point was that a PhD credential does not itself prove competence, especially when public presence or institutional connections protect weak scholarship from normal scrutiny. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=5245" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:25</a> | What exactly is Proverbs 17:8 saying about bribes? | He said Proverbs are often descriptive maxims, and this one observes that bribes open doors without endorsing bribery as good or fair. | He explained that proverbs are maxims and observations, not always direct moral endorsements. In this case, Proverbs 17:8 describes how bribes work in the real world, opening doors for the person who gives them, without saying bribery is righteous. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=5530" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:10</a> | Can he give a brief summary of Egyptian prehistory up to the Old Kingdom? | He said it is too complex to compress neatly, then sketched the long developmental sequence rather than pretending there is a simple one-line summary. | He said Egyptian prehistory is not easy to summarize because it passes through multiple periods and regional stages, including Badarian and Naqada I, II, and III. He identified the predynastic background especially with the development of separate Upper and Lower Egyptian kingdoms, though the answer was then interrupted by other chat discussion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=6506" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:26</a> | Why accept Noonan's argument for Egyptian composition of Exodus but not linguistic arguments for multiple authorship of Isaiah? | He said Egyptian loanwords in Exodus are clearer and more datable, while Isaiah authorship arguments involve more subjective linguistic judgments and have counterarguments. | He said Noonan's Exodus argument rests on Egyptian loanwords that are clearly loanwords, well attested in Egyptian literature, and datable. By contrast, the linguistic case for multiple authorship of Isaiah involves more subjective judgments about style, vocabulary, rhythm, and syntax, and he sees counterarguments on the single-authorship side. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=6617" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:17</a> | From a Christian perspective, would it be acceptable if a biblical book had multiple authors? | Yes. He said multiple inspired authors would still be compatible with Christian belief. | He said multiple authorship would not be a theological problem in itself because each author could still be inspired. In that scenario, the book would be associated with the Isaianic prophetic tradition rather than one individual author, though he personally found single authorship more compelling. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=6650" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:50</a> | Could the school of Isaiah have contained a second Isaiah? | He said it is possible, but he thinks single authorship is more likely. | He allowed the possibility of a second author within an Isaiah school, but said he regards it as less likely than single authorship. He pointed to the book's literary composition around an inflection point as one reason he sees a single-author explanation as stronger than the linguistic division argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=6862" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:22</a> | In Exodus 6:20, was the later prohibition on such unions a correction of a strange custom, with Moses ironically born from such a union? | He said the relationship language is ambiguous and may reflect clan or kinship terminology, so it is not clear the later prohibited direct relationship is actually what is happening. | He said the phrase about Amram marrying his father's sister is ambiguous because kinship terms can operate differently across cultures and clan genealogies. A direct aunt marriage would later be prohibited, but he argued the ages and clan framing make it unclear whether a direct biological relationship is intended. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=7276" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:16</a> | What is his favorite published resource from Kenneth Kitchen? | He chose Kitchen's Ramesside Inscriptions, especially because it is a core resource for Egyptologists. | He chose Kitchen's Ramesside Inscriptions because, as an Egyptologist, he finds its eight volumes of Ramesside transcriptions exceptionally useful and thorough. He acknowledged that many readers know Kitchen for On the Reliability of the Old Testament, but said the inscriptions are the go-to resource for his own field. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=7372" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:52</a> | How close is modern Hebrew to biblical Hebrew? | He said modern Hebrew is not the same language form, but a simplified descendant with reduced syntax and other streamlining. | He described modern Hebrew as a simplified form with reduced syntax, new vocabulary, and some constructions absent from biblical Hebrew. His practical illustration was that biblical Hebrew lacks the modern vocabulary and grammar needed for everyday modern tasks, even though the languages are related. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=7510" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:05:10</a> | Has he heard of the butterfly people reported during the 2011 Joplin EF5 tornado, and does he think they were angels or another kind of spirit? | He said he did not know the incident and therefore could not really comment. | He said he did not know the Joplin butterfly people story well enough to judge it. He mentioned similar folklore such as Mothman-type manifestations but ended by saying he could not comment meaningfully on this specific incident. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=7606" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:46</a> | Does he think Jesus was crucified in AD 30 or AD 33? | He leans toward AD 30, though he said the difference does not matter much to him. | He gave a brief answer: he tends to lean toward AD 30, but he does not treat the difference between AD 30 and AD 33 as a major issue. The transcript does not include a detailed argument for the preference in this answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=7636" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:07:16</a> | What is his opinion on manga, comics, or graphic novels compared with prose novels? | He said they are different forms with different strengths; he enjoys manga and graphic novels but has lately read more prose to work on attention span. | He said manga, comics, graphic novels, and prose novels each bring different things to the table. He prefers manga and graphic novels over short comics for deeper storytelling, but lately reads more prose because prose helps train attention span and creates a different imaginative experience. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=7894" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:11:34</a> | Does the obelisk represent Osiris's missing phallus? | No. He said obelisks relate to sun cults, not Osiris. | He rejected the Osiris interpretation outright. He described an obelisk as essentially a pyramid on a tower and tied it to sun temples and sun cults such as Atum-Re, not to Osiris or the Osiris myth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=7940" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:20</a> | What is his opinion on data centers? | He said they are cold, uncomfortable, dusty, and that he disliked working in them. | He said data centers are cold, uncomfortable, and dusty, drawing on his experience working in them in his twenties. He associated that work with long periods indoors, little daylight, and a generally unpleasant IT environment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=8012" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:32</a> | Which is more useful for the edification of the church, artists or scholarship? | He said both are needed: scholarship supplies the data, while the arts communicate it to the culture and to ordinary people. | He said the church needs both scholarly artists and artistic scholars. Scholarship provides data and evidence, while art and storytelling carry those truths into culture, shape moral imagination, and reach people whom technical scholarship will never reach on its own. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=8205" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:45</a> | Is it equally important to have the paleontologist and the paleo artist? | Yes. He compared it to the relationship between doctors and medical illustrators: data needs careful presentation. | He said yes and compared paleo art to medical illustration. Just as biology and medical education often depend on accurate illustrations rather than raw photographs, paleontology needs artists who can communicate scientific information visually and memorably. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=8348" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:19:08</a> | Has he considered Pope Leo XIV's Magnifica Humanitus, especially on AI and just war? | He had not looked at the just war material, found the AI concerns somewhat alarmist, and argued for a middle position on AI use. | He said he had not looked at the just-war material, but found the AI discussion too alarmist because AI is not going away. He argued for a middle position: AI can be legitimate for some tasks, such as manipulated cover assets, while he avoids using it in his writing because he dislikes what it does to his prose. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=8557" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:37</a> | What does he think of identifying Mary the mother of Jesus with the Ark of the Covenant? | He rejected the claim as bad apologetics and said it lacks support in the church fathers and the text. | He rejected the Mary-as-Ark argument as a weak analogy often used in Catholic apologetics. His objection was that the divine presence in the ark imagery is above the ark between the cherubim, not simply inside the box, so the comparison to Mary carrying Jesus does not work as claimed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=8796" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:36</a> | What is his opinion of Josephus's writings about Jesus? | He said Josephus is mostly dispassionate, with one possible interpolation, and that removing it would not change the basic historical value of the passage. | He said Josephus was not a believer in Jesus and generally reports Jesus in a dispassionate, secular historical way. He allowed that one phrase identifying Jesus as the Christ may be a scribal gloss, but said the rest is prosaic enough that the passage remains historically useful even if that phrase is removed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=9132" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:32:12</a> | Is the alleged Q source legitimate, and did it exist? | He said we do not know, and he argued that there is no direct evidence for Q. | He said the existence of Q is unknown and that there is no direct evidence for it. He criticized appeals to the Gospel of Thomas as a basis for Q, since he regarded Thomas as a late Gnostic gospel and not a reliable foundation for reconstructing such a source. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=9262" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:34:22</a> | What are his thoughts on aliens and their relationship to demons? | He said it depends on the case: some claims may be optical illusions, some alleged psychic contact could be demonic, and some abduction stories may have other explanations. | He said alien claims must be separated by type rather than all equated with demons. Some may be optical illusions, alleged psychic communication with aliens could be demonic because it resembles spirit contact, and other reports may involve trauma, counterintelligence, or other explanations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=9849" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:44:09</a> | Does disagreement about the date of the Exodus undermine someone's Christian faith? | He rejected that framing and said he is traditional on essentials of the faith even though others have treated his Exodus dating position suspiciously. | He objected to treating an Exodus-date disagreement as evidence against someone's faith. He said he affirms core Christian doctrines such as the Trinity, resurrection, and return of Christ, and that suspicion over his Exodus chronology says more about the critics than about the essentials of his faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=10009" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:46:49</a> | What is his opinion on Albert Mohler's amendment to prevent Southern Baptist Convention churches from having female pastors? | He had not read the amendment and took a middle position: male pastors should be the general rule for congregations, but some specialized ministries require women. | He said he had not read the amendment, so he would not give a direct judgment on it. His broader position was that male pastors should ordinarily lead congregations, but ministries such as women's ministry or outreach to women in shelters may require women in pastoral roles, making a blanket rule culturally and practically tone-deaf. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=10187" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:47</a> | Are there published 3D animations that look indistinguishable from 2D? | He said 3D can be made to look 2D because the final image is already a two-dimensional presentation, and much modern 2D animation uses 3D models. | He said 3D can always be made to look 2D because the viewer ultimately receives a two-dimensional image. He added that much modern "2D" animation begins with 3D models that are flattened or rendered in a 2D style, so the technical possibility is not the real issue. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=10761" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:21</a> | What is the synagogue of Satan? | He said it refers to those who actively conspire against Christ and persecute Christians, not ordinary Jewish congregations as such. | He said the phrase is not a blanket label for every synagogue or Jewish congregation. It refers to those acting as adversaries by conspiring against Christ and actively persecuting Christians, especially in the first-century setting where some communities did go out of their way to oppose the church. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=10982" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:03:02</a> | Was Akhenaten a monotheist, a henotheist, or something else? | He described Akhenaten as a monolatrous pantheist rather than a true monotheist. | He distinguished monolatry from monotheism and henotheism, then described Atenism as monolatrous pantheism. In that model everything is Aten, including people, the world, stars, and other gods, but worship is directed only to Aten rather than to a plurality of gods. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=11210" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:50</a> | Does he delete caches on his web browser, and if so, how? | Not normally; he said he sets the browser to automatic purge and otherwise leaves it alone unless space is short. | He said he does not normally clear his browser cache manually unless he is very short on disk space. His usual approach is to set the browser to purge automatically and otherwise leave the cache alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=11353" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:13</a> | Does he mean writers should use exposition dumps of information in fiction? | No. He tries to avoid info dumps and gives readers only enough information to orient them, weaving more in naturally later. | He said info dumping is not good fiction. His approach is to give readers just enough information to enter the world, allow some absence of information, and then weave additional context into the story naturally as the book progresses. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=12194" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:14</a> | With all Israel's complaints and rebellion in Numbers, would other ancient Near Eastern gods have rejected and abandoned Israel in the wilderness? | He said probably not, because Levantine idolatry often imagined gods as being bound or manipulated through idols rather than relating like the God of Israel. | He said probably not, because Levantine idolatry often treated gods as captives in idols, closer to extortion than relational worship. He contrasted that with Egyptian idols as eyes, ears, or hands of gods, saying the Levantine logic was more like holding a god hostage until it served the worshiper's aims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=12416" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:26:56</a> | Should AI be considered an attempt to usurp God's role as creator of life, and therefore blasphemy? | He said AI cannot create life; people may idolize it, but that does not make AI itself an act of creating life. | He said people can turn AI into an idol, but that is a human act of idolatry rather than AI itself creating life. He also said AI cannot create life as such; if humans ever produced artificial life, they would become responsible for it, but that would not automatically be blasphemy because human creativity reflects being made by a creative God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=13188" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:48</a> | What is the difference between soul and spirit? | He said the distinction is difficult and may be small or nonexistent in a bipartite view, though he later allowed there might be a hard-to-define difference. | He said the difference may be very small or nonexistent, depending on whether one takes a bipartite or tripartite view of human nature. He leaned away from a simple bipartite view because split-brain and communal-intelligence examples suggest a more complex internal relation between body, soul, and spirit, though he did not claim the distinction is easy to define. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=13722" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:48:42</a> | Is there a difference between a mind and a brain? | He said the mind is more or less the unity of soul and spirit, rather than simply the physical brain. | He described the mind as the unity of soul and spirit, not merely a synonym for the physical brain. He connected this to split-brain examples in which the hemispheres can operate independently but cooperatively, suggesting that mind involves more than a single undifferentiated brain process. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=14100" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:00</a> | Would mosasaurs have been intelligent in the same way as monitor lizards? | Yes. He said they would likely have similar intelligence based on reptile brain structure. | He said yes, because mosasaurs and monitors are closely related enough that similar reptile brain structure is relevant. He noted that reptile brains have bilateral hemispheres, so even if mosasaurs did not have large brains, he would expect them to fall into the same broad category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=14159" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:55:59</a> | Is the separation of mind and brain a theory? | He said it is a theory some hold, but he tends to see the mind as a function of the brain and takes an emergent-dualism position. | He said separation of mind and brain is a theory some people hold, but he does not hold simple substance dualism. He described his own view as emergent dualism, where the soul or mind develops as the brain develops and is in some sense a side effect of the brain's biological processes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=14360" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:59:20</a> | How long was the journey from Mount Sinai to Paran, and would Numbers have been short if the first generation obeyed? | He said the journey could have been only a few days for a small caravan, so Numbers would have been much shorter if Israel had obeyed. | He said a small caravan could travel from Sinai to Paran in roughly three or four days, or under five days, and that caravans could cross the Sinai in about a week at strong daily mileage. Because of that, he agreed Numbers would have been much shorter if Israel had obeyed after the time spent at Sinai. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=14532" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:12</a> | Do most denominations with female pastors or priests become theologically liberal? | He said most of them do, in his view, and treated that as a recurring pattern rather than an exception. | He said most such denominations are already theologically liberal before ordaining women, so he sees female ordination more as a symptom than a cause. In his view it tends to coincide with rejection of biblical authority and central doctrines, though he also said he has left many churches led by men for other theological reasons. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=14816" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:06:56</a> | How accurate is his history? | The subject is unclear in the transcript, but he answered "middling," saying some things are wrong and many things are right. | The transcript does not preserve the referent clearly, but he answered that the person's historical accuracy is "middling." He said some things are wrong and many things are right, so the source should be used critically rather than treated as gospel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=14856" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:07:36</a> | Why does he not like Simon the Magician? | He said Simon Magnus tried to use the Holy Spirit for profit, which is where the term simony comes from. | He said Simon Magnus tried to obtain the apostles' spiritual power so he could sell miracles for money. That attempt to use God or the Holy Spirit for profit is why the term simony is associated with him and why Falk described him so negatively. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=14954" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:09:14</a> | How religious were ancient Israelites before being scattered to Assyria and Babylon? | He said they were extremely religious, often worshiping many gods alongside Yahweh, which was precisely the problem. | He said ancient Israelites were extremely religious but often in an idolatrous way, worshiping Baal, solar deities, and other gods alongside Yahweh. He treated Elephantine as an example of Israelites worshiping Yahweh while also carrying other cults with them, which was exactly the problem the biblical critique addresses. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=15147" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:12:27</a> | Who does he think the younger lady from KV35 is, and why is her mouth the way it is? | He said the mouth is likely a post-mortem effect, not evidence of a horrible death, and that genetic evidence identifies her as King Tut's mother though her name is unknown. | He said the open mouth is probably due to post-mortem contraction during rigor or mummification, not proof of a terrible death. On identity, he said genetic tests indicate she was Tutankhamun's mother, but her name and much else about her remain unknown. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=15323" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:15:23</a> | What is gopher wood? | He said we do not know; speculative guesses lack evidence and comparative ancient Near Eastern material does not clarify it. | He said the text does not describe gopher wood clearly enough to identify it. He rejected speculative claims such as wood without tree rings as unsupported and said comparative ancient Near Eastern material does not solve the question either. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=15370" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:16:10</a> | How are the bunny and shrimp? | He said the bunny was recovering from an eye infection and the shrimp were doing fine. | He said the bunny was old, about twelve, and recovering from an eye infection. The shrimp were doing well and he hoped they would eventually start producing baby shrimp, though that had not happened yet. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=15434" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:17:14</a> | Why is the suicide rate in seminaries higher than average? | He attributed it to the difficulty and stress of seminary, isolation, and the way some students' study becomes detached from worship and faith. | He gave two main reasons: seminary work is intensely difficult and stressful, and many students become spiritually detached when the Bible becomes only an academic subject rather than part of worship. He also mentioned isolation and loss of faith, saying that when Scripture becomes a source of stress instead of blessing, the pressure becomes severe. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=15806" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:23:26</a> | Does a speech impediment automatically disqualify someone from being an evangelist? | No. He said it may make some things harder, but it can also make someone a better listener and is not a disqualifier. | He said a speech impediment does not disqualify anyone from evangelism, though it may make some aspects harder. He added that people with speech impediments can be better listeners and more careful speakers, which can actually help in evangelistic settings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=15904" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:25:04</a> | What occupation options are there after finishing seminary education? | He said many seminary graduates never become pastors and instead move into sales, corporate leadership, strategy, organization, or other roles using transferable skills. | He said seminary trains skills that transfer into corporate life, including public speaking, listening, team management, strategy, organization, language study, and even financial counseling. Many graduates become salespeople, executives, or vice presidents, though the religious label on the degree can create a bias hurdle. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/vcMbdIAWPX0?t=16321" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:32:01</a> | How did he make his speech impediment go away? | He said it did not go away; he still has it and manages it consciously. | He said he never made the speech impediment disappear; he still has it and notices it often in recordings. His strategy is to stop, pause, wait for it to subside, and then continue, while acknowledging that every person's speech pathology differs and the same approach may not work for everyone. |
